author: Eun-Ji Lee, RD, CSP, CNSC
content:
CLINICAL NUTRITION CONSULT — HOSPITAL DAY 4
Date/Time: 3/18/2027, 11:30 AM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
Referring service: Internal Medicine
Interpreter: Spanish phone interpreter present for bedside discussion.

REASON FOR CONSULT:
Severe malnutrition requiring nutritional rehabilitation plan.

NUTRITION ASSESSMENT:

Anthropometrics:
- Height: 150 cm
- Admission weight (3/15): 35.4 kg → BMI 15.7 (WHO Class III thinness, <16.0)
- Current weight (HD4, 3/18): 36.2 kg (0.8 kg up — likely fluid)
- Usual body weight (per chart 1/12/2026): 50.8 kg
- % weight change from UBW: -30.3% over 14 months
- Ideal body weight (Hamwi, 4'11"): ~45 kg
- % UBW: 69.7%

Laboratory markers (admission):
- Serum albumin 1.6 g/dL (ref 3.5-5.0)
- Prealbumin 6 mg/dL (ref 15-36)
- Total protein 4.8 g/dL (ref 6.0-8.3)
- Zinc 32 ug/dL (ref 60-110)
- Copper 62 ug/dL (ref 70-140)
- 25-OH Vitamin D 12 ng/mL (ref 30-100)
- Vitamin B12 246 pg/mL (low-normal)
- Folate 4.2 ng/mL (low-normal)
- Iron 32, TIBC 182
- Selenium: send-out pending
- Pre-feeding electrolytes (3/15): Mg 1.4, Phos 1.8, K 3.0, Ca 7.8 — all low.

Physical findings (bedside):
- Anasarca with abdominal wall edema despite cachexia
- Diffuse scaling, flaking dermatosis with 'flaky-paint' appearance over abdomen and trunk
- Diffuse scalp hair thinning with proximal-shaft pigmentary banding
- Beau's lines on multiple fingernails
- Severe muscle wasting demonstrated on CT (paraspinal, iliopsoas, gluteal, pectoralis)
- Hypopigmentation of hair (lighter than her natural dark brown)
- Mild ascites and pleural effusions on CT (hypoalbuminemic third-spacing)

Intake history (per family report): Approximately 400-600 kcal/d for past 10+ days, mostly Ensure and broth. Last solid food ~3/3/2027.

ESTIMATED NUTRITIONAL NEEDS (cautious initial approach given pre-feeding electrolyte derangements + prolonged minimal intake + BMI <16):
- Initial: 10 kcal/kg/d × 36 kg = 360 kcal/d (Day 1 goal)
- Advance 5 kcal/kg/d every 24-48 hours if K, Mg, Phos remain in safe range
- Goal maintenance: ~25-30 kcal/kg/d × adjusted weight
- Protein: start 0.6-0.8 g/kg/d (~22-29 g/d), advance to 1.2-1.5 g/kg/d once stable
- Fluids: ~25-30 mL/kg/d but conservative initially given edema; current at ~1200 mL/d total IV + meds

FEEDING ROUTE: Enteral via NG tube (placed today after MBSS demonstrated severe dysphagia with aspiration). NG preferred over parenteral: GI tract is functional, maintains gut integrity, easier transition to oral.

FORMULA: Nutren 1.0 (1 kcal/mL, 40 g/L protein) for initial period. Iso-osmolar, well-tolerated. Avoid high-density formulas initially.

FEEDING PLAN — INITIAL (HD 4-5):
Day 1 (today, 3/18/2027):
- Thiamine 100 mg IV given BEFORE feeding (already provided; documented). Also 200 mg IV at admission.
- Initiate Nutren 1.0 at 15 mL/hr continuous via NG (15 × 24 × 1 = 360 kcal/d, 14.4 g protein, 360 mL fluid)
- Multivitamin IV daily, folic acid 1 mg IV daily, thiamine 100 mg IV daily x next 3 days then PO
- Zinc sulfate 220 mg via NG TID (660 mg/day; 50 mg/day elemental) x 2 weeks
- Vitamin D 50,000 IU PO weekly x 4 weeks

Day 2 (3/19): IF K >3.5, Mg >1.7, Phos >2.5 AM labs — advance to 25 mL/hr (600 kcal/d)
Day 3 (3/20): IF labs OK — advance to 35 mL/hr (840 kcal/d)
Day 4 (3/21): IF labs OK — advance to 45 mL/hr (1080 kcal/d)
Day 5 (3/22): IF labs OK — advance to 55 mL/hr (1320 kcal/d ≈ 30 kcal/kg/d)

MONITORING:
- Q6h K, Mg, Phos for first 72 hours of advancing feeds; daily thereafter.
- Daily CMP, Mg, Phos for entire admission.
- Pre-albumin twice weekly (Mon/Thu); albumin weekly.
- Strict I/O; daily weights.
- Watch for tolerance: abdominal distension, residuals (check q4h initially), diarrhea.

ELECTROLYTE REPLETION:
- K: replete to >4.0 before any feed advance.
- Mg: replete to >1.8 (IV 2-4 g over 4-6 hr OR PO oxide 400 mg BID).
- Phos: replete to >2.5 (Phos-Nak QID PO/NG OR sodium phosphate 0.16 mmol/kg IV over 6 hr).
- Repeat 6 hr after each repletion.

FAMILY EDUCATION (today, ~25 min with interpreter):
Discussed with patient and mother:
- Reasons for NPO and NG tube.
- Why we cannot 'just feed her': cardiac arrhythmia risk if pushed too fast.
- The 1-week ramp-up plan.
- Transition pathway: NG tube now → PEG tube if dysphagia doesn't recover in 2-4 weeks → goal full oral diet over coming months.
- Expected improvements: less edema, weight gain, skin improvement, eventual hair improvement.
- Role of vitamins/minerals.

Mother asked about supplementing with traditional Guatemalan foods (caldo de pollo, atol de elote, recados) once we transition to oral. Encouraged — culturally important and nutritionally sound.

GOAL DISCHARGE:
- NG vs. PEG tube based on swallow recovery.
- Oral diet advancement as tolerated.
- Outpatient dietitian follow-up (Spanish-speaking; FQHC nutrition services).
- Coordinate with social work for ongoing food access (Maryland Food Bank, FQHC produce-Rx).

Follow-up: Daily nutrition rounding. Available for any feeding intolerance or electrolyte crisis.
attestation:
Electronically signed by: Eun-Ji Lee, RD, CSP, CNSC
Clinical Nutrition Service, Johns Hopkins Hospital
Co-signed by: Dr. Aaron Steinberg, MD (Nutrition Support Service attending)
Date: 3/18/2027
